201: Rationalist philosopher whose Ethics, geometrically demonstrated, is a cornerstone of modern philosophy.
202: German polymath and co-inventor of infinitesimal calculus alongside Newton, and a pioneer of symbolic logic.
203: French chemist, father of modern chemistry, who was guillotined during the Revolutionary Terror.
204: German mathematician known as the 'Prince of Mathematics,' with fundamental contributions to algebra, geometry, and statistics.
205: Monk and scientist, father of modern genetics, whose laws of heredity revolutionized biology.
206: Russian chemist who created the periodic table of elements, the foundation of modern chemistry.
207: The Sun King, embodiment of French absolutism, who built the Palace of Versailles and reigned for 72 years.
208: Last king of the Ancien Régime in France, guillotined during the French Revolution.
209: Queen of France and wife of Louis XVI whose perceived extravagance symbolized the excesses of the Ancien Régime; she was guillotined.
210: Jacobin leader during the French Revolution who headed the Reign of Terror before being guillotined during Thermidor.
211: French revolutionary leader who co-led the Terror before being guillotined on Robespierre's orders.
212: Radical journalist and revolutionary who was assassinated in his bathtub by Charlotte Corday during the French Revolution.
213: French general and hero of both the American and French Revolutions, a transatlantic symbol of liberty.
214: French diplomat who served the Revolution, Napoleon, and the Restoration, a master of political survival.
215: Enlightenment philosopher who formulated the separation of powers in The Spirit of the Laws, laying the foundation for modern democracies.
216: Philosopher and encyclopedist who directed the Encyclopédie, a key work of the Enlightenment.
217: French writer and feminist, author of the Declaration of the Rights of Woman, guillotined for her ideas.
218: Italian Dominican philosopher and author of The City of the Sun, a Renaissance utopia; he spent 27 years in prison for his ideas.
219: British writer and author of 1984 and Animal Farm, a sharp critic of twentieth-century totalitarianism.
220: British modernist writer and author of Mrs Dalloway and A Room of One's Own.
221: French existentialist philosopher who declined the Nobel Prize and was the intellectual partner of Simone de Beauvoir.
222: French philosopher and feminist whose work The Second Sex founded modern feminism.
223: French-Algerian writer and philosopher, Nobel Prize in Literature laureate, and author of The Stranger and The Plague.
224: Spanish poet and playwright, a central figure of the Generation of '27, assassinated at the outset of the Civil War.
225: Chilean poet, Nobel Prize in Literature laureate in 1971, and the preeminent voice of Latin American love and political poetry.
226: Austrian physicist whose wave equation is fundamental to quantum mechanics, famous for the thought experiment of the cat.
227: Italian opera composer and master of bel canto, author of Norma and La Sonnambula.
228: First Roman emperor born outside the Italian peninsula.
229: Roman emperor whose reign was notably marked by the Edict of Caracalla, extending Roman citizenship.
230: Queen of Castile and consort queen of Aragon, also known as Isabella the Catholic.
231: King of Aragon and regent of Castile; together with his wife Isabella, the first monarchs of Castile and Aragon to be called the Catholic Monarchs.
232: Roman general and statesman who reformed the army by creating professional legions; consul seven times and rival of Sulla.
233: Roman dictator and the first general to march on Rome with his legions; his proscriptions marked the end of the Republic.
234: Roman politician and general, the wealthiest man in Rome and member of the First Triumvirate with Caesar and Pompey, who crushed the Spartacus rebellion.
235: Second Roman emperor and successor of Augustus, who retired to Capri in his later years and left power in the hands of Sejanus.
236: Fourth Roman emperor who conquered Britain and expanded the Empire; poisoned by his wife Agrippina.
237: Roman emperor who destroyed the Temple of Jerusalem in AD 70 and inaugurated the Colosseum in Rome.
238: Last emperor of the Flavian dynasty who ruled with an iron fist and was assassinated in a palace conspiracy.
239: Roman emperor and one of the Five Good Emperors whose reign was a period of peace and prosperity.
240: Son of Marcus Aurelius and Roman emperor, known for his passion for gladiatorial combat; his death triggered a civil war.
241: Roman emperor who established the Tetrarchy by dividing the Empire into four parts and voluntarily abdicated to retire to his cabbage garden.
242: Last emperor of the unified Roman Empire who established Christianity as the official state religion.
243: Byzantine general in the service of Justinian I who reconquered North Africa and much of Italy for the Empire.
244: Frankish knight, leader of the First Crusade, and first ruler of the Kingdom of Jerusalem after conquering the city in 1099.
245: French monk who preached the People's Crusade that preceded the official First Crusade.
246: Holy Roman Emperor and leader of the Third Crusade, who drowned in the Saleph River.
247: Pope who convened the Fourth Crusade and the Fourth Lateran Council, bringing papal power to its zenith.
248: Spanish conquistador and the first European to sight the Pacific Ocean from the Americas (1513), later executed on the orders of Pedrarias.
249: Last great tlatoani of the Aztec Empire who received Hernán Cortés as a guest before being overthrown and dying during the Conquest.
250: Last Inca emperor before the Spanish conquest, captured and executed by Pizarro at Cajamarca.
251: Interpreter, adviser, and consort of Hernán Cortés who was a key figure in the conquest of Mexico as a cultural and linguistic intermediary.
252: Spanish Dominican friar and defender of indigenous peoples whose writings influenced the New Laws of the Indies (1542).
253: Spanish explorer who survived eight years lost in North America walking from Florida to Mexico, and was a pioneering chronicler.
254: American writer and master of the short story, pioneer of the detective tale and modern Gothic horror.
255: American poet and essayist, author of Leaves of Grass and father of American free verse.
256: American writer and humorist, author of The Adventures of Tom Sawyer and Huckleberry Finn, considered the father of American literature.
257: Irish writer, poet, and playwright, author of The Picture of Dorian Gray, icon of aestheticism and celebrated victim of Victorian puritanism.
258: French writer, father of modern science fiction, and author of Twenty Thousand Leagues Under the Sea and Around the World in Eighty Days.
259: British novelist and author of Frankenstein, or The Modern Prometheus, considered the first science fiction novel.
260: British novelist and author of Pride and Prejudice, master of social portraiture of the English rural gentry.
261: German writer and author of Faust and The Sorrows of Young Werther, a towering figure of European literature.
262: German poet and playwright, friend of Goethe, and author of William Tell and the Ode to Joy that Beethoven set to music.
263: Russian poet considered the father of modern Russian literature, killed in a duel at the age of 37.
264: Russian writer and playwright, master of the short story and modern theater, and author of The Cherry Orchard.
265: Austrian composer and pioneer of the Romantic lied who composed over 600 songs and nine symphonies in his 31 years of life.
266: German composer and music critic, a central figure of musical Romanticism and husband of the pianist Clara Schumann.
267: German composer, the fourth great 'B' of German music (alongside Bach, Beethoven, and Bruckner) and master of the Romantic symphony.
268: Italian composer and master of opera, author of Aida, La Traviata, Rigoletto, and Nabucco.
269: Italian composer and Verdi's heir in Italian opera, author of Madama Butterfly, La Bohème, and Tosca.
270: Russian composer and author of Swan Lake and The Nutcracker, master of Romantic melody.
271: French composer and pioneer of musical Impressionism, author of Clair de lune and La mer.
272: French composer, author of the celebrated Bolero and the orchestrated Pictures at an Exhibition, and master of orchestral color.
273: Russian composer whose work The Rite of Spring (1913) caused a scandal and revolutionized twentieth-century music.
274: Last German emperor whose naval ambitions and aggressive diplomacy contributed to the outbreak of World War I.
275: German general and de facto commander of the German army during World War I alongside Hindenburg.
276: German field marshal, hero of Tannenberg in 1914, and President of the Weimar Republic who appointed Hitler as chancellor in 1933.
277: American general and commander of the American Expeditionary Forces in World War I.
278: French marshal and Supreme Allied Commander in 1918 who accepted the German surrender in the Compiègne railway carriage.
279: French marshal, hero of Verdun in World War I, and head of the collaborationist Vichy regime in World War II.
280: British officer and archaeologist who led the Arab Revolt against the Ottomans in World War I, known as Lawrence of Arabia.
281: Founder and first president of the Republic of Turkey who modernized the country following the collapse of the Ottoman Empire.
282: Russian Marxist revolutionary and organizer of the Red Army, exiled by Stalin and assassinated in Mexico with an ice axe.
283: German field marshal and commander of the Afrika Korps, nicknamed the Desert Fox, who was forced to commit suicide after the plot against Hitler.
284: British field marshal who defeated Rommel at El Alamein and commanded ground forces during the Normandy landings.
285: General and 34th President of the United States who served as Supreme Allied Commander for the Normandy landings and modernized the American highway system.
286: American general and commander of the 3rd Army, an aggressive and controversial leader of armored warfare in Europe.
287: French general and president who led Free France during World War II and founded the Fifth Republic.
288: Soviet marshal who commanded the victories at Stalingrad, Kursk, and the capture of Berlin, the principal hero of the USSR in the war.
289: American general and Pacific commander in World War II and the Korean War, famous for his 'I shall return' pledge to the Philippines.
290: Japanese admiral and architect of the attack on Pearl Harbor, killed when his plane was shot down by the Americans.
291: Italian dictator, founder of fascism, and Hitler's ally, executed by the Italian resistance in 1945.
292: Spanish general and dictator who led the Nationalist side in the Civil War and ruled Spain for nearly 40 years until his death.
293: Emperor of Japan who reigned during World War II and the postwar era, renouncing his divine status after defeat.
294: Soviet leader and Stalin's successor who initiated de-Stalinization and faced off against Kennedy during the Cuban Missile Crisis.
295: Soviet leader after Khrushchev who governed the USSR for 18 years during the era of stagnation and the Cold War.
296: Last leader of the USSR whose reforms (Perestroika and Glasnost) led to the end of the Cold War and the dissolution of the Soviet Union.
297: 40th President of the United States whose aggressive policy toward the USSR contributed to the end of the Cold War.
298: British Prime Minister known as the Iron Lady who reformed the British economy and led the Falklands War.
299: Cuban revolutionary and leader who overthrew Batista and governed Cuba for nearly half a century under a communist regime.
300: Socialist President of Chile who was overthrown and killed during Pinochet's military coup in 1973.
